perturbation.original_text: In the event that DeltaThree disputes any amounts set forth on the report, DeltaThree shall provide PrimeCall with written notice of such dispute, which notice shall detail the reasons for the dispute, within seven (7) days of its receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that DeltaThree disputes any amounts set forth on the report, DeltaThree shall provide PrimeCall with written notice of such dispute, which notice shall detail the reasons for the dispute, within two (2) days of its receipt thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the dispute notification timeframe to two days is unreasonable and potentially contradicts legal standards of fair business practice, as it doesn't allow sufficient time for a thorough review of complex financial reports.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-605 states that a buyer must provide notification of rejection of goods to the seller within a reasonable time. While this contract is for services, the principle of 'reasonable time' for dispute notification is a generally accepted standard. Two days may not be considered a reasonable time to thoroughly review a financial report and formulate a detailed dispute, making it potentially unenforceable under the spirit of commercial law.

Waiver of Buyer's Objections by Failure to Particularize. (1) The buyer's failure to state in connection with rejection a particular defect which is ascertainable by reasonable inspection precludes him from relying on the unstated defect to justify rejection or to establish breach (a) where the seller could have cured it if stated seasonably; or (b) between merchants when the seller has after rejection made a request in writing for a full and final written statement of all defects on which the buyer proposes to rely. (2) Payment against documents made without reservation of rights precludes recovery of the payment for defects apparent in the documents.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the time frame for disputing amounts in a report from seven to two days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this timeframe could be deemed unreasonable under the general principles of commercial law. NY UCC § 2-605, as seen in Scraped Law Snippet 1, emphasizes the importance of a 'seasonable' time for stating defects in the context of goods rejection. Although the contract pertains to services, the legal principle of 'reasonable time' is a standard applicable across commercial contexts. Reducing the notification timeframe to two days potentially makes it difficult to thoroughly review a financial report and formulate a detailed dispute, thus contradicting the spirit of commercial law.

perturbation.original_text: Within three (3) months of the date hereof, DeltaThree shall establish and administrate a PrimeCall web site (the "PrimeCall Web Site") in consultation with PrimeCall which will enable PrimeCall to market and sell its calling cards via the World Wide Web and provide on-line support for such sales.
perturbation.changed_text: Within six (6) months of the date hereof, DeltaThree shall establish and administrate a PrimeCall web site (the "PrimeCall Web Site") in consultation with PrimeCall which will enable PrimeCall to market and sell its calling cards via the World Wide Web and provide on-line support for such sales.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the timeframe for website establishment may result in the contract not being completed within one year, potentially triggering Statute of Frauds concerns if the original intent was for shorter-term execution and performance.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-701, the Statute of Frauds, requires certain agreements to be in writing and signed to be enforceable, including agreements that cannot be performed within one year from the making thereof. Delaying the website launch to six months might push the completion of DeltaThree's obligations beyond a year, requiring the contract to strictly adhere to Statute of Frauds requirements to remain enforceable in New York.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'three (3) months' to 'six (6) months' for website establishment introduces a potential contradiction concerning the Statute of Frauds. The Law Explanation indicates that delaying the website launch may push the completion of DeltaThree's obligations beyond a year, thus requiring the contract to adhere strictly to Statute of Frauds requirements to remain enforceable in New York. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically NY GOL § 5-701, states that agreements 'not to be performed within one year from the making thereof' must be in writing. The modification creates uncertainty as to whether the contract can be completed within one year, potentially triggering this requirement and creating a legal contradiction if the contract doesn't meet those requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Any amounts due hereunder shall be calculated and paid in U.S. dollars on a monthly basis within twenty-five (25) business days following the receipt of the reports detailed in Sections 3.06 [Reporting] and 4.02 [Reporting] hereof or any other invoices provided for herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Any amounts due hereunder shall be calculated and paid in U.S. dollars on a monthly basis within sixty (60) business days following the receipt of the reports detailed in Sections 3.06 [Reporting] and 4.02 [Reporting] hereof or any other invoices provided for herein.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment timeframe to 60 business days (approximately three calendar months) may be deemed commercially unreasonable and could violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under New York law.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 1-304 states that every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. While this contract may not be explicitly governed by the UCC, the principle of good faith applies to all contracts in New York. A 60-business day payment term, without justification, could be seen as commercially unreasonable and a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, especially if it places undue burden on one party.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment timeframe to 60 business days, which the perturbation explanation suggests may be deemed commercially unreasonable and could violate implied duties of good faith. The law explanation also indicates that a 60-business day payment term, without justification, could be seen as commercially unreasonable. This aligns with the definition of inconsistencies creating a legal contradiction when a time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements, specifically when a contract sets a deadline that violates state law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state, 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement'. Therefore, extending the payment timeframe without justification may contradict the obligation of good faith.
